ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised soya sauce chicken; mapped to the broader 'chicken dish' class as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for braised soya sauce chicken; A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Braising in soya sauce is a preparation variant not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for braised soya sauce chicken as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not routinely code composite prepared dishes at this specificity.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 1 chicken thigh or drumstick with sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised soya sauce chicken is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one chicken thigh or drumstick with a small amount of braising sauce, as commonly served in Singaporean hawker centres and home cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Soya Sauce Chicken — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Braised Soya Sauce Chicken (F11020012). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and sodium are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked chicken thigh (USDA #05165) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate braised chicken dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised soya sauce chicken. The dish contains only 0.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making a formal GI measurement impractical. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) by analogy with other high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked chicken preparations documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 0.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.09, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 295 mg per 100 g from soya sauce braising
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100 g per meal, request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparation, and avoid consuming the braising sauce or gravy to minimise sodium intake, as soy sauce braising can deliver higher effective sodium than raw nutrient data suggests.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.4 g/100 g) and high protein (23.9 g/100 g) with negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised soya sauce chicken is well-suited for blood glucose management due to its negligible carbohydrate content and high protein density; individuals with type 2 diabetes should request minimal-sugar preparation of the braising sauce and remove the skin to reduce saturated fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium (295 mg/100 g) and moderate total fat (8.77 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~2.5 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, consume skinless portions only to reduce saturated fat, limit portion to 100–120 g, avoid the braising sauce to reduce sodium load, and pair with high-potassium vegetables to support a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised soya sauce chicken (酱油鸡, Jiang you ji) is a classic Singaporean Chinese dish in which whole chicken pieces — typically thighs, drumsticks, or a whole bird — are slow-braised in a fragrant sauce of dark and light soy sauce, sugar, garlic, ginger, star anise, and cinnamon. The prolonged braising process renders the chicken tender and deeply flavoured, with the skin taking on a rich mahogany glaze. The dish is a staple of Singaporean hawker centres, home kitchens, and Chinese New Year celebrations, and is closely related to Teochew and Hokkien braised poultry traditions. Nutritionally, it is high in protein (23.9 g/100 g) and moderate in fat (8.77 g/100 g), with very low carbohydrate (0.4 g/100 g) and low sugar (0.3 g/100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. Sodium is 295 mg/100 g, which is a moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management; consuming the dish without the braising sauce significantly reduces sodium intake. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker and restaurant preparations. It is commonly served with steamed white rice or plain porridge, and is a popular lunch and dinner option across all age groups in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11020012) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken thigh data as an adaptation, documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for very-low-carbohydrate chicken dishes. All other fields are grounded in approved sources.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked chicken thigh (USDA SR Legacy #05165) as the HPB Foodo database does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. This is an approved-source adaptation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this specific braised preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. The input record incorrectly included 'plant_based' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as the dish contains chicken. 4. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for braised soya sauce chicken was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. The very low carbohydrate content (0.4 g/100 g) makes formal GI measurement impractical and glycemic load is negligible. 5. Dietary fibre (0.75 g/100 g) is sourced from HPB Foodo and reflects minor contributions from braising spices and sauce ingredients.
